item.title: Substance under analysis
item.description: A PACKAGE containing a white powdery substance, reportedly marked with the letters “DHL” and recovered from Namena Island in Cakaudrove, has been sent to Suva for analysis. Police spokeswoman Ana Naisoro said the package, containing a white substance believed to be illicit drugs, was found washed ashore and recovered by police.
item.content:encoded:
A PACKAGE containing a white powdery substance, reportedly marked with the letters “DHL” and recovered from Namena Island in Cakaudrove, has been sent to Suva for analysis.

Police spokeswoman Ana Naisoro said the package, containing a white substance believed to be illicit drugs, was found washed ashore and recovered by police.

Ms Naisoro said officers from the Savusavu Police Station were deployed on Tuesday, July 7, after receiving reports of the discovery.

“The substance is yet to be tested. It is being brought to Suva,” she said.

The parcel was retrieved by crime scene investigation officers.

She thanked members of the public for promptly reporting suspicious packages.

She said 76 parcels recovered to date from the Lau Group, Qamea, Kadavu and Beqa had all tested positive for cocaine.

“The Fiji Police Force is liaising with the Australian Federal Police to ascertain the source and destination of the illicit commodities.”

Members of the public have been urged not to handle suspicious packages but to report them immediately by calling 1681.

Meanwhile, the Northern Division’s Operation Sasamaki team seized more than 5600 plants believed to be marijuana from a farm in the Qaravutu farming area.

A suspect has been taken into custody for questioning and investigations are continuing.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Police officers nab vehicle thief
item.description: A police vehicle was damaged after officers used it to block a suspected stolen car during an arrest in Wailoku last Saturday. Police said the vehicle had allegedly been stolen from Davuilevu Housing on Thursday, July 16, prompting a lookout notice to be issued to officers.
item.content:encoded:
A police vehicle was damaged after officers used it to block a suspected stolen car during an arrest in Wailoku last Saturday.

Police said the vehicle had allegedly been stolen from Davuilevu Housing on Thursday, July 16, prompting a lookout notice to be issued to officers.

The vehicle was first sighted in Lami before it was tracked to Wailoku, where officers from the Eastern Division Crime Intelligence Unit, Southern Division Operations and Quick Response Teams intercepted it.

“The suspect tried to flee the scene; however, the police vehicle was used to block his vehicle,” police said in a statement.

The police vehicle sustained damage during the operation.

The suspect, who police said has a known criminal history, remains in custody as investigations continue.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Carnival committee secures sponsor
item.description: THE Duavata Northern Crime Prevention Carnival has secured a three-year sponsorship commitment from Flour Mills of Fiji Ltd. This is a major boost to the annual event which aims to promote crime prevention and community awareness.
item.content:encoded:
THE Duavata Northern Crime Prevention Carnival has secured a three-year sponsorship commitment from Flour Mills of Fiji Ltd.

This is a major boost to the annual event which aims to promote crime prevention and community awareness.

Carnival committee president Satish Kumar announced the sponsorship during the launch of the carnival in Labasa.

Mr Kumar said the agreement followed numerous discussions.

“We will be signing the agreement soon. Last year FMF supported us in a smaller way, but this year they have come on board in a much bigger way for the FMF Duavata Northern Crime Prevention Carnival 2026,” he said.

The carnival is more than a fundraising event, he said, with contestants serving as ambassadors to educate communities on crime, particularly the growing threat of illicit drugs.

“The biggest threat we face today is drugs. These contestants will help educate people throughout the Northern Division. We proudly call ourselves the Friendly North, and we want to keep it that way.”

He said this year’s major community project would be the construction of a classroom at Valelawa in Dreketi where some students were still studying in tents.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Man jailed for larceny
item.description: A FORMER employee of the Fiji Sugar Corporation (FSC) convicted of obtaining a financial advantage and falsification of documents has been sentenced to one year and 11 months. Roneel Chand was found guilty by Magistrate Amelia Korodrau. In 2019, Chand intentionally and dishonestly intercepted and converted to his personal use a grant cheque valued at $1300 that was intended for a farmer.
item.content:encoded:
A FORMER employee of the Fiji Sugar Corporation (FSC) convicted of obtaining a financial advantage and falsification of documents has been sentenced to one year and 11 months.

Roneel Chand was found guilty by Magistrate Amelia Korodrau. In 2019, Chand intentionally and dishonestly intercepted and converted to his personal use a grant cheque valued at $1300 that was intended for a farmer.

He was employed as a section team leader with FSC and was directly involved in the application, processing, and assessment of sugar cane farmer grant applications.

The court also found that he falsified work orders to facilitate fraudulent grant payments for non-existent farm work.

Delivering the sentence on July 6, Magistrate Korodrau said the evidence established that Chand used his knowledge of FSC procedures and his years of experience to manipulate the system, deceive sugarcane farmers, and obtain a financial benefit.

He said the offending not only impacted FSC but also directly affected sugar cane farmers, who were particularly vulnerable and relied heavily on grant assistance.

Given the seriousness of the offence, the court found there was no justification for a suspended sentence.

Prosecutor Joshua Francis Prasad appeared on behalf of FICAC, while Chand was represented by William Lomaloma from Legal Aid.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Education Ministry calls for student-centered decision-making
item.description: EVERY decision made in Fiji’s education system should be judged by one measure — whether it improves the learning and wellbeing of students, says the Ministry of Education. Speaking on behalf of Education Minister Aseri Radrodro at the School Management Association of Fiji’s 11th National Conference in Suva, deputy secretary Timoci Bure called on school leaders, management committees and education
item.content:encoded:
EVERY decision made in Fiji’s education system should be judged by one measure — whether it improves the learning and wellbeing of students, says the Ministry of Education.

Speaking on behalf of Education Minister Aseri Radrodro at the School Management Association of Fiji’s 11th National Conference in Suva, deputy secretary Timoci Bure called on school leaders, management committees and education stakeholders to remain united in putting children first.

“We may come from different schools, different organisations and different communities, but we are united by one purpose, to give every child the opportunity to succeed through education,” Mr Bure said.

He said every child had the right to learn in a safe and supportive environment, while teachers and school leaders also needed strong backing from their communities.

“Every child deserves the opportunity to learn in a safe, supportive and well-managed school.

“Every teacher deserves the support needed to teach effectively. Every principal deserves the confidence of knowing they are supported by their School Management Committee and their community.”

Mr Bure urged delegates attending the conference to ensure that discussions and decisions remained focused on students.

“As you participate in this conference, I ask that you keep one question in mind: How will this improve the learning and wellbeing of our students?

“Every discussion, every decision and every investment in education should be measured against that question.

“When we keep students at the centre of our decisions, we remain focused on what matters most.”

The conference brought together school management representatives, education leaders and stakeholders from across the country to discuss ways of strengthening school governance and improving educational outcomes.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: BACK IN HISTORY | Patel’s venture thrives
item.description: AT just 27 years of age, Sikh Video Craft owner Ravi Patel was transforming a personal interest in video production into a growing business venture. The Fiji Times reported on Saturday, May 14, 1994, that Mr Patel invested $20,000 into the business and was beginning to see the returns from his commitment.
item.content:encoded:
AT just 27 years of age, Sikh Video Craft owner Ravi Patel was transforming a personal interest in video production into a growing business venture.

The Fiji Times reported on Saturday, May 14, 1994, that Mr Patel invested $20,000 into the business and was beginning to see the returns from his commitment.

“I started this business because I was interested in video production,” he said.

“At first it was just a hobby but then I saw there was a market for it.”

His company, based in Nadi, specialised in producing Fijian videos, music videos and documentaries, with demand for locally made content increasing.

“We’ve been getting a lot of requests from schools and organisations to produce videos for them.

“People want to see themselves on TV and on video.”

Mr Patel employed two staff members at the time and had ambitions to expand the operation. He was also producing videos for overseas audiences, particularly Fijian communities in Australia, New Zealand and the US.

“The overseas market is big for us. We’ve sent videos to Fijian communities in Australia, New Zealand and the US. They want to see what’s happening back home.”

While the business was growing, Mr Patel said access to equipment remained one of the biggest challenges.

“Video equipment is very expensive. But if you’re serious about the business, you have to invest.”

Looking back on his progress, Mr Patel said determination and belief were essential ingredients for success.

“Hard work does pay off in the end. You just must believe in what you’re doing.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Fisheries budget pivots to long-term growth
item.description: The Ministry of Fisheries is implementing strict internal austerity to pivot resources toward long-term aquaculture development, research infrastructure, and strategic support for local resource owners. The ministry received an increased allocation of $28.4million in the 2026-2027 National Budget – an increase of $3.83m compared to the allocation in the 2025-2026 fiscal year of $24.6m.
item.content:encoded:
The Ministry of Fisheries is implementing strict internal austerity to pivot resources toward long-term aquaculture development, research infrastructure, and strategic support for local resource owners.

The ministry received an increased allocation of $28.4million in the 2026-2027 National Budget – an increase of $3.83m compared to the allocation in the 2025-2026 fiscal year of $24.6m.

Responding to the 2026-2027 National Budget in Parliament last week, Minister for Fisheries and Forestry Alitia Bainivalu said the increased allocation reflected the Government’s commitment to developing fisheries as a key contributor to the economy.

Ms Bainivalu said the increase represented a 1.6 per cent growth, which she added was a clear signal of the Government’s confidence in the fisheries sector as a driver of sustainable economic growth and to support the country’s well-being.

She said the increase had been strategically planned to support resource owners and guide future investment.

“This increase is not arbitrary,” Ms Banivalu said.

“It reflects a strategic forecast and provides confidence and direction for investing in our resource owners.”

Ms Bainivalu said while the ministry’s operating budget had been reduced, the focus was on improving efficiency and directing more resources towards long-term development.

“Operating expenditure has been reduced by $581,200 (down by 5.1 per cent), requiring tighter controls on wages, allowances, travel, and maintenance.”

She said capital investment had risen significantly to strengthen the sector’s resilience.

“Capital expenditure has increased by $4,685,300 (up by 47 per cent), prioritising on infrastructure, research facilities, and grants (offshore Fisheries and Aquaculture), that will strengthen long-term resilience and productivity.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: BUSINESS OPINION | Taxed under criminal threat | Fiji’s FNU training levy betrayal
item.description: For six years, Fiji’s employers paid a levy under criminal threat — fines, imprisonment for non-payment — collected specifically, by law, to train their own workers. For six of those years, the government kept 90 per cent of that money for purposes the levy was never created to fund, without employer consent and without meaningful recourse. That is not a funding dispute. It is a broken promise enf
item.content:encoded:
For six years, Fiji’s employers paid a levy under criminal threat — fines, imprisonment for non-payment — collected specifically, by law, to train their own workers. For six of those years, the government kept 90 per cent of that money for purposes the levy was never created to fund, without employer consent and without meaningful recourse.

That is not a funding dispute. It is a broken promise enforced by criminal penalty, and it deserves to be named as one before anyone credits this month’s partial fix as the end of the story. This month’s budget restores half of what was taken. It does not restore what those six years actually cost — in skills never built, in $FJ8000 paid per worker recruited from overseas because no local one was trained, and in the 95 job categories still sitting empty today because the fund built to fill them was, for years, spent on almost anything else.

The levy began in 1973 under the Fiji National Training Council, funded by a compulsory 1 per cent deduction from every registered employer’s payroll, enforceable by law with fines and potential imprisonment for non-payment. For 45 years, the arrangement held: employers paid under threat of prosecution, and in return, every dollar trained their workers. The Fiji National Training Levy Order 1988 formalised that link explicitly, leaving no ambiguity about what the money was for. The scheme survived two institutional transformations — into the Training Productivity Authority of Fiji in 2003, then into Fiji National University’s National Training and Productivity Centre in 2011 — without breaking that promise once. It broke in 2018.

The 2018–19 Budget redirected the levy without employer consent. Under the new formula, half of all collections went to a Government General Practitioners scheme, 40 per cent to the Accident Compensation Commission Fiji, and only 10 per cent remained for the training the levy legally exists to fund. Employers were still prosecutable for non-payment of a levy now spent overwhelmingly on healthcare and compensation schemes they had no say in creating. That is the injustice at the centre of this piece: coercion retained in full, purpose abandoned by nine-tenths.

Between 2019 and 2024, Fiji’s businesses contributed approximately $FJ152.4 million to the levy. Less than $FJ$15.2m reached actual training grants. Roughly $FJ137m was redirected elsewhere. In 2022 alone, about $FJ23m was collected against just $FJ2.3m released for training. The average employer, according to the Fiji Commerce and Employers Federation, recovered roughly $FJ376 a year — about $FJ7 per employee — from a fund they were legally compelled to fund at gunpoint of criminal law.

The National Skills Gap Assessment Survey identified 95 occupations where employers cannot find suitably qualified local workers. Businesses report spending roughly $FJ8000 to recruit a single worker from overseas — a cost that dwarfs the training grant most employers ever recovered. In 2024, the United Nations flagged concern over Fiji’s progress on Sustainable Development Goal 8, decent work and economic growth. A scheme built specifically to prevent this outcome was, for six years, funded at a tenth of its intended level while the shortage it existed to prevent grew steadily worse.

The regulator problem underneath the money

Beneath the funding dispute sits a structural conflict rarely discussed with the same intensity as the dollar figures. Section 35A(2)(a) and (d) of the FNU Amendment Decree 2010 gives Fiji National University’s own National Training and Productivity Centre regulatory authority over the very training sector FNU itself competes in as one of the country’s largest providers. FNU collects the levy, assesses grant applications, sets regulatory standards, and runs training programmes — collector, referee, and leading player, all in one institution. One Fiji Times opinion piece put it plainly last October: no honest legal reading can call that regulatory function independent of FNU.

This month’s 2026–27 National Budget finally moved. Finance Minister Esrom Immanuel announced the Training Grant Scheme allocation rises from 0.1 per cent to 0.5 per cent of the levy — a fivefold increase — funded through the phased withdrawal of the Private GP Scheme, whose existing trust fund covers the transition. The Accident Compensation Commission Fiji retains its 0.4 per cent. A new allocation, 0.1 per cent, now funds the Fiji Learning Institute for Public Service.

Call this what it is: real, and still nowhere near enough. Half the levy now reaches training, against the roughly ninety percent that reached it for the scheme’s first 45 years. The new 0.1 per cent allocation to FLIPS, moreover, funds public service training specifically — a legitimate public interest, but not the private-sector workforce development employers were coerced into funding since 1973. Dr Hasmukh Lal, welcoming the reform, has already called for the next step: an independent body drawing on the Higher Education Commission, the Fiji Commerce and Employers Federation, and the Public Service Commission, to take grant-claimable course approval out of FNU’s hands entirely.

The reform has opened a question nobody was asking before it. Cabinet has separately decided government may access levy funds for its own training purposes, prompting a pointed response from Fiji Higher Education Commission Director Dr Eci Naisele: with most of the levy already redirected for years, and now government itself drawing on what remains, what happens to the private-sector employers who are still the only ones legally compelled to pay it under threat of prosecution?

Section 29 of the Fiji National Training Act establishes a National Training Appeals Tribunal, empowered to hear challenges to levy administration decisions. Constitutional questions concerning statutory purpose and legislative authority over compulsory collections remain open to judicial interpretation. That employers pursued advocacy for seven years rather than testing this in court says something about how confident they were of a remedy through either channel.

Three things would complete what this budget has only started. First, separate the regulatory function from FNU entirely. Second, clarify in law, not merely by Cabinet decision, exactly how much of the levy the government itself may access. Third, commit to a public timetable for closing the remaining gap between 0.5 percent and full restoration.

A government that compels payment under criminal threat for one stated purpose, then redirects nine-tenths of it for six years without consent and calls a fivefold increase back to half of the original figure a solution, has set a precedent every other compulsory levy in this country should now be measured against. The training levy’s history should stand as the reference case for how long an injustice can persist, in plain sight, funded by force of law, before it is even partially undone.

Dr Sushil K Sharma BA MA MEng (RMIT) PhD (Melbourne) is a World Meteorological Organisation (WMO) Accredited Class 1 Professional Meteorologist. He is also a former Associate Professor of Meteorology at FNU. The views expressed are solely his and do not necessarily reflect the views of this newspaper.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
